steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The hypothesis is that Ag-NP bioaccumulation in P. pruinosus can serve as a proxy for X. laevis — meaning measurements in the invertebrate must reliably predict measurements in the frog. A weakening finding must break that predictive link.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — P. pruinosus has been shown to accumulate several other types of manufactured nanoparticles in addition to Ag-NPs, whereas X. laevis has been shown to accumulate only Ag-NPs.: Describes a breadth difference but says nothing about whether Ag-NP levels in one species predict levels in the other. ✗
  • Choice B — Compared with X. laevis, P. pruinosus can accumulate detectable Ag-NP concentrations with significantly fewer negative effects.: Addresses tolerance, not whether concentrations correlate. ✗
  • Choice C — Ag-NP concentrations in P. pruinosus are not predictive of Ag-NP concentrations in X. laevis when the two species are in environments with comparable levels of Ag-NPs.: Directly severs the predictive link the proxy depends on — if the invertebrate's readings don't forecast the frog's readings, the proxy is worthless. ✓
  • Choice D — When P. pruinosus and X. laevis are exposed to similar levels of Ag-NPs, concentrations of Ag-NPs in animals of both species do not show substantial variation from individual to individual.: Low within-species variance would actually support the proxy hypothesis by making readings more reliable. ✗
That is the correct answer.
